coredata.dc:title: Biobased carbon dots production via hydrothermal conversion of microalgae Chlorella pyrenoidosa

coredata.dc:description: A promising green hydrothermal process was used to produce biobased nanomaterials carbon dots (CDs) by using microalgae Chlorella pyrenoidosa (CP) and its main model compounds (i.e., glucose, glycine, and octadecanoic acid). The possible reaction pathway including hydrolysis, Amadori rearrangement, cyclization/aromatization, and polymerization was first proposed for the hydrothermal process to produce microalgae-based CDs. Interactions among carbohydrates and proteins in microalgae were vital intermediate reactions in the generation of CDs. The mass yield of CDs reached 7.2% when the CP was hydrothermally treated with 20:1 of liquid-to-solid ratio at 230 °C for 6 h. It was confirmed that nitrogen, sulfur, phosphorous, and potassium were doped onto CP-based CDs (CD-CP) successfully without additional reagents or treatments. The CD-CP yield was 4.0–24.3 times higher than that of model compound-based CDs. Regarding morphology, CD-CP was constituted by many spherical nanoparticles smaller than 20 nm. These CDs emitted blue fluorescence under ultraviolet light, and the fluorescence quantum yield of CD-CP was 4.7–9.4 times higher than that of CP model compound-based CDs. Last, CD-CP displayed broad application prospects as a sensor for Fe3+ detection in wastewater with high sensitivity.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A promising green hydrothermal process was used to produce biobased nanomaterials carbon dots (CDs) by using microalgae Chlorella pyrenoidosa (CP) and its main model compounds (i.e., glucose, glycine, and octadecanoic acid). The possible reaction pathway including hydrolysis, Amadori rearrangement, cyclization/aromatization, and polymerization was first proposed for the hydrothermal process to produce microalgae-based CDs. Interactions among carbohydrates and proteins in microalgae were vital intermediate reactions in the generation of CDs. The mass yield of CDs reached 7.2% when the CP was hydrothermally treated with 20:1 of liquid-to-solid ratio at 230 °C for 6 h. It was confirmed that nitrogen, sulfur, phosphorous, and potassium were doped onto CP-based CDs (CD-CP) successfully without additional reagents or treatments. The CD-CP yield was 4.0–24.3 times higher than that of model compound-based CDs. Regarding morphology, CD-CP was constituted by many spherical nanoparticles smaller than 20 nm. These CDs emitted blue fluorescence under ultraviolet light, and the fluorescence quantum yield of CD-CP was 4.7–9.4 times higher than that of CP model compound-based CDs. Last, CD-CP displayed broad application prospects as a sensor for Fe3+ detection in wastewater with high sensitivity.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), novel zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials, are composed of carbon skeletons with sp2/sp3 hybrid carbon nuclei and are rich in hydroxyl or carboxyl functional groups (Abbas et al., 2018; Mou et al., 2021), which are biocompatible, soluble in water, and easily functionalized (Xiao et al., 2016). Meanwhile, they usually display oxidation-reduction ability and electron transfer/storage capacity (Park et al., 2018; Yeston, 2017). Since CDs were discovered accidentally in the carbon nanotube electrocatalysis process (Xu et al., 2004), they have been applied in many fields, such as biosensors (Tang et al., 2022), environmental monitoring (Guan et al., 2017), water treatment (Zhao et al., 2017), drug delivery (Paul and Kurian, 2021), cellular imaging (Guo et al., 2017), catalysis (Feng et al., 2020), energy storage (Hoang et al., 2019), photoelectric conversions (Olmos-Moya et al., 2022), anti-counterfeiting (Tao et al., 2021), and foliar application (Yang et al., 2022).

CDs are commonly prepared by a top-down approach or a bottom-up approach (Boruah et al., 2020). The latter approach has become more attractive if cheap and renewable biomass materials provide widely available carbon sources (Kurian and Paul, 2021). The biomass hydrothermal method, as a general bottom-up approach, has the merits of simple operation and strong controllability and can oxidize and passivate CDs simultaneously (Yang et al., 2011). Moreover, water and biomass are used, while chemical reagents are rarely introduced during the hydrothermal process (Mehta et al., 2015). Generally, this environmentally friendly method has become a hotspot for CDs production in recent years (Kurniawan et al., 2021; Lin et al., 2021).

Lignocellulose is the most frequently used biomass carbon precursor of CDs. For example, green fluorescence emitting CDs were synthesized successfully when hydrothermally treated empty fruit bunches at 200 °C for 12 h (Park et al., 2018). Some previous studies have found that the nitrogen (N)-containing groups enhance CDs light conversion ability (Wang et al., 2021), the sulfur (S)-containing groups improve CDs catalytic performance (Lv et al., 2017), and the phosphorous (P)-containing groups redshift CDs emission fluorescence wavelengths (Park et al., 2018). Therefore, to make lignocellulose-based CDs special characters, researchers have devoted much work to modifying CDs with chemical reagents, such as ethanediamine (Chen et al., 2019) and ammonium persulfate (Shen et al., 2017). However, the addition of reagents might lead to cost increases and pollution problems (Hu et al., 2017), which forebodes a demand in searching for appropriate self-dope able CD feedstocks.

Microalgae are natural biomass abundant in N, P, and S elements, are widely distributed, and exhibit a short growth cycle with high photosynthetic efficiencies (Hepburn et al., 2019; Li et al., 2022). Microalgae feedstock can be sourced from natural water blooms or massive cultivation, which shows a high potential to prepare heteroatom-doped CDs and is especially suitable for hydrothermal systems (Zhang et al., 2017). In addition, the small size of microalgae brings them large contact areas to take part in reactions even without precrushing, and the ignorable amount of lignin makes their structures vulnerable to destruction by heating. However, to the best of our knowledge, systematic research on the production of CDs by using microalgae and the formation mechanism of microalgae-based CDs have not yet been reported.

The present work prepared CDs by microalgae Chlorella pyrenoidosa (CP) and its main model compounds (glucose/GLU, glycine/GLY, and octadecanoic acid/OCT) through a hydrothermal process for the first time. The aims of this study are to:
•
Investigate the regularity and reaction pathway of the CD formation process from the perspective of CP model compounds.
•
Characterize CDs properties and potential application in wastewater pollutant detection.
•
Design an appropriate scheme for CDs production by exploring hydrothermal parameters.

CP powder (32.2% carbohydrate, 48.9% protein, and 13.4% lipid based on dry weight) was obtained from Fuqing King Dnarmsa Co., Ltd. (China). The hydrothermal conversion of CP was conducted in a 100-mL hydrothermal autoclave with heating and stirring (Prasannan and Imae, 2013). The CP and water (mass ratio of liquid to solid/mL:mS: 5:1–30:1) were added to the autoclave and mixed adequately. Nitrogen gas was injected into the autoclave for several minutes to replace the inside atmosphere completely before the reactor was sealed. The autoclave was heated to the stated reaction temperature (T: 150–270 °C) and kept for the designed reaction time (t: 2–10 h). Then, the autoclave was cooled quickly after the reaction was finished. The products were removed and centrifuged at 8000 r/min for 20 min. The upper liquid in the centrifuge tube was weighed (w 1) and transferred into a dialysis bag (1000 Da). After dialyzing for 24 h to remove small molecules (Xu et al., 2004), the residual solution in the dialysis bag was freeze-dried for 48 h and weighed (CD/carbon dots: w 2). The solid inside the centrifuge tube (w 3) was weighed, and sufficient ethyl acetate (AR, Shanghai Titan Technology Co., Ltd., China) was used to extract organics from the solid (Yang et al., 2014). The organic liquid was moved out, and ethyl acetate was removed by rotary evaporation. The residual solid was dried at 80 °C for 12 h and weighed (HC/hydrocarbon: w 4). GLU, GLY, or OCT (AR, National Pharmaceutical Group Co., Ltd., China) was also used as feedstock to conduct hydrothermal conversion with the same procedure. The CD yield and hydrothermal product percentages (AQ/aqueous product, OR/organic product, HC, CD, and VO/volatile product) were calculated by the following equations:

S are the weights of water and feedstock used in the hydrothermal reaction, w 1 is the total weight of AQ and CD, w 2 is the weight of CD, w 3 is the total weight of OR and HC, and w 4 is the weight of HC. In addition, the obtained products are referred to as X or X-Y in the following text, where X represents the type of product AQ, OR, HC, CD, or VO, and Y distinguishes the species of carbon precursor CP, GLU, GLY, or OCT.

To analyze C, H, N, and S contents, CP and CD were tested with Unicube elementar (Elementar Analysensysteme GmbH, Germany). The O content was obtained from the difference calculation (Zhang et al., 2021). FT-IR tests of feedstock, HC, and CD were performed on a Nicolet iS50 spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) using the potassium bromide tableting method. Gold-coated CP or HC was tested by a TESCAN VEGA3 scanning electron microscope (SEM) (Tescan Co., Ltd., Czech Republic) at an accelerating voltage of 20 kV. CD was analyzed by Talos F200S transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Netherlands) to confirm its morphology and elemental characteristics. A NanoBrook Omni nanometer particle size analyzer (Brookhaven, USA) was used to test the CD size distribution in its aqueous solution.

The obtained CD solution was diluted 10, 20, 30, 40, or 50 times and was denoted CD-Y-10, CD-Y-20, CD-Y-30, CD-Y-40, or CD-Y-50. The CD solution was used to test the UV–visible absorption spectrum by an ultraviolet−visible spectrophotometer (Persee, China) and run for fluorescence excitation spectrum by an FLS1000 stable transient fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments, UK) with excitation wavelengths of 300–500 nm.

AQ and OR were tested by GCMS2010–TQ8040 (Shimadzu, Co., Ltd., Japan, Rtx WAX column: 30 m × 0.25 mm × 0.25 μm) to confirm their components. The test program was 37 °C (held for 7 min) to 250 °C (held for 10 min) with a 3 °C/min heating rate, while the injector was kept at 250 °C in a 50:1 split ratio using helium as the carrier gas. The compounds were identified using the NIST14 and NIST14s mass spectra libraries.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of metal ions in wastewater by CD-CP
ce:section.ce:para: A set of 1 mmol/L metal ion (Mg2+, Zn2+, Na+, Ca2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, K+, Co2+, Mn2+, Pb2+, Fe3+ and Fe2+) solutions was prepared by MgSO4·7H2O, ZnSO4·7H2O, Na2SO4, CaCl2, NiCl2·6H2O, CuSO4, K2SO4, CoCl2, MnSO4, Pb(CH3COO)2, FeCl3 and FeSO4·7H2O. A 1 mL ion solution was mixed with 4 mL CD-CP-100 (CD-CP solution diluted 100 times) and took place at room temperature for 30 min. Then, the mixture was run for fluorescence excitation spectroscopy with excitation at 360 nm. Subsequently, the selected ion was used to explore the relationship between ion concentration and fluorescence intensity (Zhao et al., 2020). The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated based on the three times standard deviation rule (LOD = 3σ/s, where σ represents the blank standard deviation, and s is the slope of the calibration curve) (Shen et al., 2017).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of hydrothermal parameters on the production of CD-CP
Mass ratio of liquid to solid
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 1a, the yield of CDs increased when mL:mS was enhanced from 5:1 to 25:1 and then decreased at 30:1, which indicated that increasing the solvent promoted CD production. However, excessive water might reduce the reactivity of raw materials during the hydrothermal process. Fig. 1b shows that the C content in CDs increased when mL:mS was enhanced from 5:1 to 10:1, decreased between 10:1 and 20:1, and fluctuated slightly between 20:1 and 30:1. The changing O content in CDs appeared contrary to the C content, while its S content tended to increase, and the N and H contents changed slightly without apparent regularity when mL:mS was altered. It was confirmed that the CD elemental composition was affected when mL:mS was lower than 10:1 because insufficient solvents led to many side reactions (such as deoxidization) (Arun et al., 2021). The slight variation in elemental compositions between 20:1 and 30:1 mL:mS suggested that enough solvent had been added to the hydrothermal system.

In addition to CDs, CP hydrothermal conversion produced AQ, HC, and VO products, which could be used as bio-oil, biochar, and biogas with high added value (Leng et al., 2020; Liu et al., 2020). In Fig. 1c, AQ increased, while OR, CD, and VO decreased with increasing mL:mS, as less precursor was used during the reaction. HC declined to 0.2% when mL:mS was increased to 20:1 and changed slightly at approximately 0.2–0.3% between 20:1 and 30:1 mL:mS. It indicated that the enhancement of solvent accelerated CP degradation in the hydrothermal process (Zhang et al., 2019b); however, the acceleration became nonexistent when mL:mS was greater than 20:1 and too high mL:mS might cause the requirement of more solvent and energy.

The yields of CDs under different hydrothermal temperatures (see Fig. S1a) declined at higher temperatures as degradation reactions to produce low molecular weight products were promoted. Regarding the CD elemental composition (see Fig. S1b), the S content increased while the H content remained stable with increasing temperature. The N and C contents increased first (150–190 °C for N content, 150–230 °C for C content) but decreased at higher temperatures, whereas the O content displayed a trend opposite to the N content when the temperature was changed. The results indicated that a higher temperature accelerated carbonization, N doping, or S doping of CDs. In comparison, N doping or carbonization acceleration became adverse when temperatures were higher than 190 °C or 230 °C. Based on this finding, to make the CDs a higher C content, 230 °C is considered the most suitable temperature to prepare microalgae-based CDs.

As to the product distributions under different temperatures (see Fig. S1c), AQ increased between 150 and 210 °C, HC and CD decreased between 150 and 270 °C, and OR increased while VO decreased between 150 and 250 °C. It was found that a higher temperature promoted CP degradation to generate organics, but that a surplus temperature might accelerate gas production. The variation in HC or OR displayed regularity similar to that of paunch waste hydrothermal carbonization (Marzbali et al., 2021) or wastewater algal hydrothermal liquefaction (Carpio et al., 2021) when the reaction temperature was changed.

As shown in Fig. S2a, the CD yield increased when the reaction time was prolonged to 6 h and decreased slightly between 6 and 10 h. This phenomenon illustrated that excessive time might cause repolymerization or cracking of CDs and lead to a lower CDs yield. C and H contents in CDs increased when prolonging time to 10 h, N content increased until 4 h and decreased between 4 and 10 h, and S content increased between 2 and 8 h but decreased at 10 h. In contrast, O content decreased as reaction time was extended (see Fig. S2b). It is considered that carbonization and deoxidization of CDs were promoted when the time was prolonged to 4 h, changed slightly between 4 and 8 h, and increased again with the time longer than 8 h. Based on CDs yield and elemental composition, 6 h was the proper reaction time for preparing microalgae-based CDs.

The product distribution of hydrothermal processes at different times fluctuated without apparent regularity (see Fig. S2c), which implied that reaction time complexly influenced the CP hydrothermal process. The HC changed slightly with changing time, which is consistent with Chlorella vulgaris hydrothermal carbonization (Khoo et al., 2020).

The optimal CD-CP (obtained at 20:1 mL:mS, 230 °C temperature, and 6 h time) contained 56.8%, 6.3%, 8.1%, 1.2%, and 27.6% C, H, N, S, and O, which verified that N and S had been doped onto CD-CP successfully. The N and S contents were approximately 7.8% N but lower than 4.2% S in aqueous ammonia and sulfuric acid-doped Syzygium cumini fruit extract-based CDs (Dhanush and Sethuraman, 2021). Furthermore, their contents were similar to 9.0% N and 1.0% S in cyanobacteria Spirulina hydrothermal CDs (Zhang et al., 2019a), demonstrating that the CP hydrothermal process could dope N and S onto CDs without extra auxiliary reagents.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Production of CP model compound-based CDs
ce:section.ce:para: CP and its model compounds (GLU, GLY, and OCT) were hydrothermally treated under optimal conditions to further explore the mechanism of CD hydrothermal production. As shown in Fig. 2a, the yield of CD-CP reached 7.2%. It was approximately 11.4, 4.0, or 24.3 times higher than that of CD-GLU, CD-GLY, or CD-OCT, which implied that interactions among microalgae main components induced the generation of hydrothermal CDs. In particular, almost no CD-OCT was produced, which indicated that OCT was not a feasible precursor to produce hydrothermal CDs. The feedstock yield into CD is often omitted due to its low value (Oliveira and Silva Abreu, 2021), making this technology less economical and practical. Compared with other studies, the CD-CP yield was 9.6 times higher than the macroalgae Enteromorpha prolifera-based CD yield (0.75%) (Wang et al., 2019).

Regarding the product distribution in Fig. 2b, GLU generated vast OR, VO, and HC. GLY produced a relatively high content of CD, and most OCT remained after hydrothermal conversion. Additionally, the GLY hydrothermal process brought the most AQ, and the hydrothermal conversion of GLU generated maximum HC and VO. At the same time, only some VO was produced during the OCT hydrothermal process. The interaction among the main CP compounds accelerated the formation of CD, which might contribute to the amidation of fatty acids with amino or Maillard reactions between reducing sugars and amino acids (Fan et al., 2020). Generally, CP is an outstanding CD precursor and shows a higher CD yield than conventional carbon precursors.

ce:section.ce:section-title: FT-IR of feedstocks, CDs, and HCs
ce:section.ce:para: FT-IR spectra of feedstocks, CDs, and HCs are shown in Fig. 3 , and characteristic peak affiliations are listed in Table S1. The OCT and CD-OCT displayed almost the same spectrum except that CD-OCT involved an OH peak at approximately 3420 cm−1, indicating that CD-OCT was likely to be OCT dissolved in water. These CDs had higher percentages of multiple associative OH at 3230 cm−1 than the feedstocks and HCs (Zhang et al., 2020), which signified many combinable bands around hydroxyl groups. Peaks at approximately 2955 cm−1, 2920 cm−1, and 2850 cm−1 correspond to stretching vibrations of CH from CH2, and CH3 (Yang et al., 2014). The CD-CP displayed less OH but more CH than CP did, CD-GLU contained fewer OH and CH bands than GLU, and CD-GLY appeared to have more OH but less CH than GLY does. It is assumed that interactions among CP components promote the saving of CH bonds in CDs.

In the CP system (Fig. 3a), the free CO peak at 1735 cm−1 tended to move to the bonded CO peak at 1696 cm−1 (Prasannan and Imae, 2013) in CD-CP or HC-CP. The CD-CP contained more aromatic structures because the stretching vibrations of aromatic rings (1650 cm−1 and 1550 cm−1) in CD-CP appeared stronger than those in CP and HC-CP. For the GLU system (Fig. 3b), CD-GLU and HC-GLU contained more CO and aromatic rings than GLU, indicating the occurrence of condensation and aromatization during the GLU hydrothermal process. In terms of the GLY system (Fig. 3c), unsaturated bonds appeared less in CD-GLY than in GLY because each GLY molecule had one CO bond.

Generally, CD-CP appeared to have more CH than CD-GLU and CD-GLY, increasing unsaturated bands than CD-GLU, but less OH and unsaturated bands than CD-GLY. Peaks at 3008 cm−1 and 1522 cm−1 (asymmetric stretching and bending vibration of NH bond) (Mou et al., 2019), peaks at approximately 1300–1000 cm−1 (stretching vibration of CO) (Yang et al., 2011), and peaks at 1119 cm−1, 701 cm−1, and 620 cm−1 (asymmetric stretching and bending vibration of SO4 2−) (Zhang et al., 2020) appeared and verified the presence of N/O/S elements in CD-CP, which could make CD-CP excellent hydrophilicity and high dispersibility in aqueous solutions for environmental application (Gai et al., 2019).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and size distribution of CDs
Morphology of CP, HC-CP, and CDs
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 4a–c, many small round clustered CPs with sizes of 4–30 μm were transformed into irregularly shaped HC-CP blocks with sizes of approximately 1–70 μm and subglobular CD-CP particles smaller than 20 nm. The shape of HC-CP is irregular, unlike the spherical hydrocarbon obtained from the glucose hydrothermal reaction alone (Sheng et al., 2020). It should be noted that some particles of approximately 100 nm were also discovered in CD-CP, which might correspond to small hydrocarbon or giant molecular nanocarbon produced from repolymerization. The size of CD-CP appeared larger than 1.5–4.0 nm of plant petal hydrothermal-derived CDs (Shi et al., 2015), similar to 20–50 nm of CDs obtained from the hydrothermalism of Spirulina (Zhang et al., 2019a), but smaller than 67 nm of Pectinodesmus sp. hydrothermal CDs (Amjad et al., 2019). The morphology indicated that microalgae hydrothermal conversion could produce water-soluble and subglobular particles at the nano level, but these particles were slightly larger than traditionally defined CDs.

When comparing Fig. 4d–f, CD-GLU was composed of many 40 nm particles, while CD-GLY was made up of irregular branches connected by many nanometers of matter. This finding confirmed that interactions among the main components affected the morphology of microalgae-based CDs. Furthermore, EDS results (Fig. 4h–l) indicated that O, potassium (K), P, N, and S had been doped onto CD-CP directly from the CP hydrothermal process, which proved that using CP as a precursor had significant advantages in preparing multielement-doped CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Size distribution of CDs in aqueous solution
ce:section.ce:para: The sizes of CDs in their solutions were several times larger than their solid particles (Fig. 5 ). However, the size of crop waste hydrothermal CDs in the solution is in good accordance with the TEM result (Ding et al., 2021). Due to the larger size and more surface functional groups, the obtained CDs in this study might exhibit more substantial hydration or chelation effects in water and display a larger hydrodynamic diameter (Liu et al., 2009). Another study found that cyanobacteria Spirulina hydrothermal CD in its solution appeared six times larger than solid microscopy, which was supposed to neutralize surface charges and destabilize the colloidal system by high salinity CD solution (Zhang et al., 2019a).

In Fig. 5a, most of the CD-CP in an aqueous solution showed a diameter of approximately 10–100 nm and some appeared to be 200–500 nm. While most CD-GLU and CD-GLY wherein aqueous solutions showed diameters of 200–500 nm and 150–200 nm, respectively. In addition, some 1000 nm particles appeared in CD-CP or CD-GLY solution and were assigned to water-soluble polymers produced from the hydrothermal process. Moreover, the CD-OCT aqueous solution consisted of particles larger than 1000 nm. This might be OCT existed in water and again indicated that OCT hydrothermal conversion had not produced CD. During the dynamic light scattering test, a larger particle size shows a larger surface, a higher light intensity, and a greater volume. Therefore, the above finding was confirmed again in Fig. 5b–d. When comparing these four solutions, CD-CP displayed the smallest average particle size, consisting of many 10–100 nm particles and bits of particles with diameters approximating those of CD-GLU and CD-GLY. It is implied that interactions among carbohydrates and proteins promote the generation of smaller CD particles, while lipids are hardly involved in producing CDs.

As shown in Fig. 6 , CD solutions emitted blue fluorescence under UV light at 365 nm, which is similar to the Spirulina pyrolytic CD solution (Agnol et al., 2021). CD-CP displayed the most intense fluorescence, while CD-OCT showed the slightest fluorescence. In more detail, the fluorescence of the CD solution changed obviously with decreasing CD concentration. CD-CP solution exhibited an aggregation-induced fluorescence quenching phenomenon, ascribed to the direct π-π interaction or excessive resonance energy transfer among aggregated CD-CP in its high concentration solution (Han et al., 2021). It has been confirmed that the concentrations of CD in CD-CP, CD-GLU, CD-GLY and CD-OCT solutions were 1.18 mg/mL, 0.35 mg/mL, 0.66 mg/mL and 0.19 mg/mL, respectively. The blue fluorescence of the CD-CP solution appeared to grow, while the fluorescence of the other three CD solutions tended to weaken when the solution dilution factor was increased. The fluorescence quenching phenomenon in the CD-CP solution faded away with CD-CP dilution, while a lower CD concentration in other solutions generated weaker fluorescence. When comparing CD-CP-50, CD-GLU-20, CD-GLY-30, and CD-OCT-10, which contained approximately 0.02 mg/mL CD, the CD-CP-50 displayed a stronger blue fluorescence than other three solutions. Therefore, CP hydrothermal treatment was proven to produce more CDs with powerful fluorescence characteristics than those produced by GLU, GLY, or OCT hydrothermal processes.

In the UV–visible absorption spectra of CD-CP-50, CD-GLU-20, CD-GLY-30, and CD-OCT-10 (see Fig. S3), broad absorptions were observed between 200 and 500 nm, except that almost no peak appeared in CD-OCT-10. The peak at approximately 205 nm in CD-GLU-20 or CD-GLY-30 is attributed to π-π* transitions of aromatic constituents (Eda et al., 2010; Mahat and Shamsudin, 2020). The CD-CP-50 exhibited a redshift of this peak to 238 nm, which might be related to the higher electron delocalization and lower transition energy of its conjugated system. The 292 nm peak in CD-CP-50, which corresponded to CO group n-π* transitions (Mahat and Shamsudin, 2020), exhibited a higher intensity and larger wavelength than the corresponding peak at 263 nm in CD-GLU-20 and CD-GLY-30. Another peak at 365 nm corresponding to the n-π* transitions of N/P/O-containing groups (Olmos-Moya et al., 2022; Tao et al., 2021) appeared to have a similar trend. It is assumed that CD-CP contains more N/P/O-containing groups than CD-GLU and CD-GLY, with higher intensities of peaks at 292 nm and 365 nm. Generally, the structure of CD-CP-50 showed a higher conjugation degree and included more heteroatomic groups than CD-GLU, CD-GLY, or CD-OCT. CD-CP's UV–visible absorption spectrum appeared to be similar to that of graphitic carbon nitride-based 2D nanosheets (Pareek et al., 2020), indicating the existence of CO and CN structures in CD-CP.

The fluorescence emission spectra of the obtained CDs mainly depended on the excitation wavelength and displayed a bathochromic shift when the excitation wavelength was increased (Fig. 7 ), which might be attributed to the surface state and bandgap of N/P/O-doped CD (Boruah et al., 2020). Fluorescence emission wavelengths with maximum photoluminescence (PL) intensities of these CDs are 440 nm, 430 nm, 410 nm, and 410 nm with excitation at 360 nm, 340 nm, 340 nm, and 300 nm. Again, this result confirmed that these CDs emitted fluorescence in the blue or blue-violet region when excited by UV light. Specifically, the emission peak of CD-CP was redshifted from 440 nm to 550 nm between 300 and 500 nm excitation wavelengths. CD-GLU, CD-GLY, and CD-OCT solutions showed similar regularity as well. The redshift was attributed to different energy levels associated with surface states formed by various functional groups and became emissive upon surface passivation (Mehta et al., 2015). The PL intensity was enhanced when the excitation wavelength was increased from 300 nm to 360 nm for CD-CP, to 340 nm for CD-GLU or CD-GLY, but declined when the excitation wavelength was persistently increased. However, the PL intensity of CD-OCT always declined with increasing excitation wavelength.

Moreover, the photoluminescence quantum yields (PLQYs) of CD-CP, CD-GLU, CD-GLY, and CD-OCT were 7.9%, 1.7%, 1.2%, and 0.8%, respectively, which verified that CD-CP was superior to convert light energy. Generally, the CD-CP displayed stronger fluorescences at larger excitation wavelengths and showed better photoluminescence properties over wider excitation wavelengths. When compared with other biobased CDs, the PLQY of CD-CP was higher than that of sugarcane bagasse-based CD (4.45%) (Boruah et al., 2020) and plant petal hydrothermal CDs (1.2–6.5%) (Shi et al., 2015).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reaction mechanism in the hydrothermal process to prepare CDs

ce:section.ce:para: The identified compounds with relative peak areas higher than 5% in AQ or OR from the GC–MS test are listed in Table 1 . The main compounds of AQ-GLU were organic acids obtained from dehydration, decarboxylation, and deacetylation reactions (Xu et al., 2018). In addition to levulinic acid and acetic acid, OR-GLU was mainly composed of ethanol obtained from reduction reactions. The compounds in AQ-GLU and OR-GLU (Table S2) implied a plausible CD-GLU generation pathway deduced in Fig. S4. First, GLU and organic acid were cyclized to produce ketones and furan derivatives (such as 3-methyl-1,2-cyclopentanedione and 1-(2-furanyl)-ethanone). Next, the ketones and furan derivatives are rearranged and deoxidized to form aromatics (such as 2,4,5-trimethyl-benzaldehyde and 2,4-di-tert-butylphenol) (Chen et al., 2015). Last, CD-GLU was generated from the polymerization of these aromatics.

Regarding the GLY system, AQ-GLY mainly contained amides, amines, ketones, and acetic acids, which were produced from intermolecular condensation, decarboxylation, and deamination of GLY (Guo et al., 2015; Sato et al., 2004). These compounds cyclized and generated annular diketones and pyridine derivatives (such as dihydro-1,3-dimethyl-2,4(1H,3H)-pyrimidinedione and 3-methyl-pyridine) (Yang et al., 2017). Then, aromatics (such as 2,4-di-tert-butylphenol and 2,4,5-trimethyl-benzaldehyde) were formed from rearrangement, deoxidization, or denitrification of the above cyclic compounds (GC–MS results of the GLY system in Table S3). Then, these aromatic compounds were polymerized to form CD-GLY. The reaction paths corresponding to the above conjectures are shown in Fig. S5.

In terms of the CP system (Fig. 8 ), intricate reactions occurred during the hydrothermal process because of the complex composition of CP. First, (non)reducing sugars, amino acids, and fatty acids were produced from the hydrolysis of carbohydrates, proteins, and lipids (Yang et al., 2017). Second, Amadori rearrangement of sugars and amino acids generated ketones and amines (such as DL-alanyl-l-leucine and (3S,6S)-3-butyl-6-methylpiperazine-2,5-dione). Meanwhile, amidation reactions occurred between amino acids and fatty acids to form amides and long-chain alkanes (such as octadecanamide) (Chen et al., 2015; Xu et al., 2018). Third, sugars, amino acids, and Amadori products were cyclized, rearranged, deoxidized, or denitrified to generate aromatics (such as 2,4-di-tert-butylphenol and indole) (GC–MS results of the CP system in Table S4). Finally, these aromatics copolymerized to generate CD-CP through polymerization. Above all, four sets of reactions are speculated to occur for forming CDs: hydrolysis, Amadori rearrangement, cyclization/aromatization, and polymerization. The interactions among the main CP compounds were significant in producing cyclic compounds in the generation of microalgae-based CD.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of metal ions in wastewater by CD-CP
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 9 , most typical ions in wastewater had no obvious influence on the CD-CP fluorescence spectra, except that only Fe3+ had a significant quenching effect on the PL intensity. The F/F0 (where F and F0 are the PL intensities at 440 nm with and without the addition of metal ions) of most ions was approximately 0.96–1.08, while the F/F0 with the addition of Fe3+ was as low as 0.39. This selectivity arose because Fe3+ in water combines with hydroxyl or amino groups on the surface of CD-CP, thus destroying the radiation transition and bringing a fluorescence quenching phenomenon (Shen et al., 2017). Furthermore, the PL intensity gradually decreased when Fe3+ concentration (CFe 3+) was enhanced from 0 to 200 μmol. In particular, CFe 3+ and F0/F displayed good linear relationships (F0/F = 7.07893 × 10−6CFe 3+ + 1.13687, 1.6–200 μmol, R2 = 0.9966; and F0/F = 9.53251 × 10−5CFe 3+ + 1.00001, 0–1.6 μmol, R2 = 0.9647). The LOD of CFe 3+ was as low as 0.55 nmol/L, which was much lower than the 5.4 μmol/L iron permissible limit in drinking water prescribed by the World Health Organization (Singh et al., 2021) and 355.4 nmol/L pine wood-based CDs (Zhao et al., 2020), demonstrating the excellent ability of CD-CP to identify Fe3+ in wastewater. Generally, detection of Fe3+ by the above optical method is cost-effective and convenient, which could contribute to human bodies and natural water environments because an imbalance of Fe3+ can cause diseases (such as diabetes and cancer) and microbial death (Qi et al., 2019).

The hydrothermal process with 20:1 mL:mS at 230 °C for 6 h was identified as an optimal means of producing high-yield N, S, P-doped CD-CP with excellent properties, and the obtained CD-CP could detect the Fe3+ content in wastewater with high sensitivity. CD-CP was generated through hydrolysis, Amadori rearrangement, cyclization/aromatization, and polymerization reactions from the main CP components during the hydrothermal process. It was confirmed that interactions among carbohydrates and proteins in CP promoted the CD-CP formation, with a higher mass yield reaching 7.2%, smaller particle sizes of approximately 20 nm, a stronger fluorescence emitting ability, and a higher PLQY of up to 7.9%.
